skill.name: linkerd-patterns
skill.description: Implement Linkerd service mesh patterns for Kubernetes, including installation, mTLS, traffic policy, canary routing, multicluster setup, and observability workflows.
skill.summary: Apply practical Linkerd patterns for secure Kubernetes service mesh operations.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported 30 issues, but most backtick and weak cryptography alerts are false positives from Markdown fences and ordinary text. The confirmed risk is a pipe-to-shell installer and cluster-changing Linkerd or kubectl examples that require user review before execution. No evidence found of malicious intent, credential exfiltration, or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Remote Installer Piped to Shell
medium_findings.description: The install template downloads a script from the Linkerd installer URL and pipes it directly to sh. The URL appears to be the official Linkerd installer and uses HTTPS flags, so this is not evidence of malicious intent. It remains risky because users may execute remote code without inspection.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pipe-to-shell pattern is directly present on line 70. Context shows a legitimate Linkerd install command, which lowers confidence that it is malicious.

medium_findings.title: Cluster-Changing Command Examples
medium_findings.description: The skill includes Linkerd and kubectl examples that install CRDs, apply control plane manifests, install extensions, link clusters, and label services. These commands are appropriate for the skill domain, but they can modify live Kubernetes clusters if copied without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are visible and semantically tied to Linkerd operations. They are not hidden execution, but they can affect production infrastructure.

low_findings.title: Markdown Fence False Positives
low_findings.description: Static analysis classified multiple Markdown code fences and inline code spans as Ruby or shell backtick execution. These are documentation delimiters in SKILL.md, not executable Ruby code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged locations are Markdown fences or inline code markers. No Ruby execution context exists in the file.

low_findings.title: Expected Network References
low_findings.description: The hardcoded network references are an official Linkerd installer URL, an example API server address, official documentation links, and a private CIDR example. They do not show data exfiltration or unauthorized network access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All network references are visible documentation examples. The only execution-relevant network reference is already covered by the pipe-to-shell finding.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts do not identify cryptographic code. Line 3 is skill metadata, and line 39 is an architecture diagram label, not a DES or MD5 implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines contain descriptive text and a diagram label. No hash, cipher, or cryptographic API usage is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Install Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The install instructions pipe downloaded content into sh. This pattern should be reviewed before publication because it can execute remote code in the user's environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present. The source URL appears legitimate, so this is a safety risk rather than confirmed malicious behavior.

content.value_statement: Kubernetes teams need repeatable Linkerd patterns without rebuilding every manifest from scratch. This skill guides installation, traffic policy, authorization, routing, monitoring, and multicluster workflows.
actual_capabilities: Guides Linkerd control plane installation and validation steps.
Provides namespace and deployment injection patterns.
Explains ServiceProfile routes, retries, timeouts, and retry budgets.
Shows TrafficSplit patterns for canary deployments.
Covers Server and ServerAuthorization policy examples.
Includes monitoring and debugging command references.
limitations: It does not inspect a live Kubernetes cluster by itself.
Commands must be reviewed before use in production environments.
Examples use generic names that require adaptation.
Version-specific Linkerd behavior should be checked against current documentation.

use_cases.title: Install a Service Mesh
use_cases.description: Plan a basic Linkerd installation with validation, CRD installation, control plane deployment, and optional visualization.

use_cases.title: Add Reliable Traffic Policy
use_cases.description: Create service profile, retry, timeout, and traffic split patterns for safer application releases.

use_cases.title: Design Mesh Authorization
use_cases.description: Model server policies and client authorizations for service-to-service access control.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Linkerd Install
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan a Linkerd installation for a Kubernetes cluster. Include prerequisites, validation steps, and rollback considerations.

prompt_templates.title: Inject an Application
prompt_templates.prompt: Show how to enable Linkerd injection for my application namespace and deployment. Explain what I should verify after rollout.

prompt_templates.title: Create Traffic Policy
prompt_templates.prompt: Design Linkerd service profile and traffic split guidance for a canary release with retries, timeouts, and route metrics.

prompt_templates.title: Review Mesh Security
prompt_templates.prompt: Review a Linkerd authorization design for a production namespace. Identify risky access patterns and suggest safer policy boundaries.

output_examples.input: I need to install Linkerd in a new cluster.
output_examples.output: A staged installation plan with prerequisite checks, control plane steps, verification commands, and production review notes.

output_examples.input: I want a safer canary rollout for one service.
output_examples.output: A traffic policy outline that separates stable and canary backends, defines route behavior, and lists rollout checks.

output_examples.input: I need to restrict which services can call an API.
output_examples.output: An authorization design that defines the server, allowed client identities, ingress exceptions, and validation steps.

best_practices: Run Linkerd validation checks before and after every mesh change.
Review installer scripts and cluster-changing commands before execution.
Use service profiles, retry budgets, and timeouts together to avoid retry storms.
anti_patterns: Do not copy production commands without checking the target context.
Do not allow broad unauthenticated access without a documented boundary.
Do not enable retries for unsafe write operations without application review.

faq.question: Does this skill deploy Linkerd automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. A user or automation system must run approved commands.

faq.question: Is this skill only for new clusters?
faq.answer: No. It also helps with injection, traffic policy, monitoring, debugging, and authorization updates.

faq.question: Can it help with zero trust networking?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers Linkerd mTLS concepts and authorization policy patterns for service access control.

faq.question: Does it validate my current Linkerd version?
faq.answer: No. It references patterns from the skill file. Check current Linkerd documentation for version changes.

faq.question: Can it create canary deployment guidance?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes TrafficSplit and route policy guidance for controlled rollout planning.

faq.question: What should I review before using its commands?
faq.answer: Review the installer source, cluster context, namespace targets, service names, and production change controls.
